metadata.title: Giving Actionable Feedback to Students
metadata.summary: A ~12-minute module on making feedback specific and actionable — read the principles, then practice rewriting feedback in a scenario and get formative AI coaching. General educator PD.

content.body:
Feedback helps learning most when it answers three questions for the student: Where am I going? (the goal or success criteria), How am I doing? (against that goal), and Where to next? (the concrete next step). This 'feed up / feed back / feed forward' framing comes from Hattie & Timperley's widely cited review, The Power of Feedback (2007).

In practice, that means feedback is specific, tied to a criterion, and names ONE next move the student can act on — not vague praise. 'Nice start!' feels good but tells a student nothing. 'Your opening states a clear claim; next, add one piece of evidence to support it' tells them exactly what to do.

A useful rule of thumb (Dylan Wiliam): good feedback should be more work for the receiver than the giver — it should cause thinking and a next action, not just a grade. Keep it specific, kind, and oriented to the next step.
keyPoints: Answer: where am I going, how am I doing, where to next?
Be specific and tie it to a success criterion — not vague praise.
Name ONE concrete next step the student can act on.

content.scenario:
A 7th-grader submits the opening paragraph of an argument essay. It states a clear claim but gives no supporting evidence yet. You were about to write: "Nice start!"

Rewrite your feedback so it is specific, ties to a success criterion, and gives ONE concrete next step — while staying encouraging.
content.rubric: Specificity (names what the student actually did); ties to a criterion (e.g., claim/evidence); exactly ONE clear, actionable next step; encouraging, respectful tone; concise.

content.prompt: Think of an assignment you will give feedback on this week. Write a sentence frame you could reuse that names a strength AND one concrete next step.
